blocks.text: Chapter IV: The Persistence of Form
blocks.label: Chapter Title

blocks.text: Type design is one of the few disciplines in which the practitioner must simultaneously satisfy two entirely opposed demands: absolute invisibility and total presence. The reader must not see the type — only the ideas it carries — and yet the type must be so precisely made that any deviation from its invisible ideal would be felt immediately, as a stumble is felt in an otherwise smooth walk.
blocks.label: Body §1

blocks.text: The question of what constitutes an optimal reading experience has occupied typographers for five centuries without resolution. Gutenberg's 42-line Bible established conventions for text composition — column width, leading, type size — that remain largely intact in contemporary book design. We have changed the materials; we have not changed the proportions.
blocks.label: Body §2

blocks.text: The unit of typographic measurement — the point — was standardised in France in 1737 by Pierre Simon Fournier, though his system was not universally adopted until the nineteenth century.¹ Today, digital type is measured in points, pixels and ems simultaneously, a fact that would have bewildered Fournier entirely.
blocks.label: Body §3

blocks.text: ¹ The Didot point, adopted widely on the continent, differs from the Anglo-American point by approximately 0.015 mm — a difference invisible to the eye but significant in the composition of long texts at small sizes.
blocks.label: Footnote

blocks.text: See also: Bringhurst, R. The Elements of Typographic Style (4th ed.). Hartley & Marks, 2012. · Hochuli, J. Detail in Typography. Éditions B42, 2008.
blocks.label: References
